body:
I'm trying to use d3 to draw a network graph. And my origin dataset is like this(json):

```
{
    "nodes":[
        {
            "id": "00M",
            "name": "Thigpen"
        },
        {
            "id": "00R",
            "name": "Livingston Municipal"
        }
    ],
    "links":[
        {
            "source": "00M",
            "target": "00R",
            "opacity": 0.61
        }
    ]
}
```

And through this, I can code like below to generate a network graph **with given nodes and links:**
```
d3.json("./air.json",` function( data) {
        var link = svg
        .attr("class","links")
            .selectAll("line")
            .data(data.links)
            .enter()
            .append("line")
            .style("stroke", "#000000")
            .style("stroke-width",1)
   
        var node = svg
            .selectAll("circle")
            .data(data.nodes)
            .enter()
            .append("circle")
            .attr("r", 1)
            .style("fill", "#000000")


        var simulation = d3.forceSimulation(data.nodes)                 
            .force("link", d3.forceLink()                               
                    .id(function(d) { return d.id; })                     
                    .links(data.links)                                    
            )
            .force("charge", d3.forceManyBody().strength(-400))         
            .force("center", d3.forceCenter(width / 2, height / 2))     
            .on("end", ticked);

        function ticked() {
            link
                .attr("x1", function(d) { return d.source.x; })
                .attr("y1", function(d) { return d.source.y; })
                .attr("x2", function(d) { return d.target.x; })
                .attr("y2", function(d) { return d.target.y; })
                .style("opacity",function(d) {return (Math.sqrt((d.source.x-d.target.x)*(d.source.x-d.target.x)+(d.source.y-d.target.y)))/1200; });
                // (Math.sqrt((d.source.x-d.target.x)*(d.source.x-d.target.x)+(d.source.y-d.target.y)))/300
            node
                .attr("cx", function (d) { return d.x; })
                .attr("cy", function(d) { return d.y; });
        }
```


This seems normal, but I'm looking for a larger dataset at https://snap.stanford.edu/data/. And the dataset doesn't like the dataset I have shown before. **It only has edge lists without nodes** like the pictures(.csv):

title: Apparent case of Chicken and Egg when updating nodes
body:
I'll preface with a disclaimer that I might be using the library wrong.  Still, it would be helpful to know what the expected answer to this problem is.  Roughly what I'm doing:

1. Have a `var simNodes` that contains the nodes used for the sims.
2. Have a `var simLinks` that contains the links used.
3. Remove a node (let's call it `node11`) from simNodes along with any simLinks entry that references it.
4. Call `simulation.nodes(simNodes)` to remove the node from the simulation.
5. Call `simulation.force("links").links(simLinks)` to remove the forces that were acting on that node.
6. Call alpha and restart to kick the graph into action.

After a bunch of debugging I discovered that the problem I'm running into is that the objects in `simLinks` have `source` and `target` defined as getters instead of just references.  When a force that acted on `node11` was evaluated by the simulation the getter  would return the entry in `simNodes` for `node11` ... which is fine if the node exists, but those links are getting evaluated after I removed the node and that's a problem.  I thought I had accidentally left a link somehow but couldn't find a source.  Much digging later I found this note:

> [#](https://github.com/d3/d3-force#simulation_nodes) simulation.nodes([nodes]) · [Source](https://github.com/d3/d3-force/blob/master/src/simulation.js)
> 
> If nodes is specified, sets the simulation’s nodes to the specified array of objects, initializing their positions and velocities if necessary, and then [re-initializes](https://github.com/d3/d3-force#force_initialize) any bound [forces](https://github.com/d3/d3-force#simulation_force); returns the simulation.

Re-initializes the forces?  Eh?  Further down, forceLink says...

> When the link force is [initialized](https://github.com/d3/d3-force#force_initialize) (or re-initialized, as when the nodes or links change), any link.source or link.target property which is not an object is replaced by an object reference to the corresponding node with the given identifier.

So, if I'm understanding all this correctly, updating either the nodes or links causes an evaluation of the source and target of all links, which in this case means step 4 triggers access of the links before I can update them in step 5.  If I flip those two steps, so I update the links before nodes, I'll hit the same issue when adding nodes instead.  If I have a change set that both adds and removes nodes I'd get the error regardless of which I tried to update first.

I know the obvious answer here is to not use a getter but I'd prefer to keep them if possible.  Another obvious answer would be to return a dummy object, but that feels event worse.  Looking at the code, though, it seems like the same issue exists when links are referenced by identifier like in the second quote...

https://github.com/d3/d3-force/blob/main/src/link.js#L58
```    for (i = 0, count = new Array(n); i < m; ++i) {
      link = links[i], link.index = i;
      if (typeof link.source !== "object") link.source = find(nodeById, link.source);
      if (typeof link.target !== "object") link.target = find(nodeById, link.target);
      count[link.source.index] = (count[link.source.index] || 0) + 1;
      count[link.target.index] = (count[link.target.index] || 0) + 1;
    }```

It seems to me that if initialize gets called with links where the source or target are identifiers, and that identifier refers to a node not in the sim, find throws an error and the call to links() fails.  Since the property is overwritten it shouldn't be an issue when removing nodes, no lookup is done in that case.  It would be a problem if you tried to add to the simulation though, specifically when adding the link before adding the node.

Thoughts?  Is there a sensible way to do this without making `.source` and `.target` be objects?  Is there supposed to be an order for updating the nodes and forces?

For the time being I'm using a work around of calling `simulation.force("links").links([])" before updating the node list in step 4, so as to change the node list while there's no links present, but that's not exactly optimal.

title: Philosophical questions about virtual DOM rendering and typescript
body:
Hello! Thanks for d3js. I have always been a big fan.

I was wondering if there is any plan to update docs or examples to use typescript and/or to shift the focus away from DOM iteration since virtual DOM rendering has all but taken over the web?

For tasks that require complex mathematics and visualizations, I’m not aware of a better library, but nowadays we can get the best of both worlds if we offload some of the work to a web framework. DOM traversal is passé and, perhaps more importantly, inefficient.

There are quite a few articles and tuts that illustrate how to get the best of both worlds, and I personally think [this is the best](https://wattenberger.com/blog/react-and-d3) I have read of them so far.

I’m also curious about Typescript. The source code is (necessarily) quite idiomatic and not that easy to read, but it seems like typescript could give readability quite a boost. It is true the ‘@types/d3’ package is for the most part technically sufficient, but in the interest of maintaining the library in the long term, it seems like a logical next step to move the library into ts files.

Thanks in advance!

title: Interpolating between GeoJSON polygons (isochrones)
body:
Just trying to get a sense of what the right approach is to this problem: I've got a point _P_ that is between two isochrones (polygons _I_ and _J_, representing say 50-minute walking time and 60-minute walking time from some origin _O_, respectively). I'd like to be able to estimate a more exact walking time to _P_ based on its relative distance between the isochrones.

![untitled (14)](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/03efb124-4070-4475-92b3-095ea3f71e1a)


One thing that I could do is iterate through all the vertices in _I_ to find the two closest to _P_, and similarly for _J_. Now I've got distance from closest edge in _I_ (call it _D<sub>I</sub>_ :shrug:) and distance from closest edge in _J_ (_D<sub>J</sub>_), and could do: _D<sub>I</sub>_ ÷ (_D<sub>I</sub>_ + _D<sub>J</sub>_). Does that sound right? Is there anything in `d3` that would help with this calculation?

Alternatively maybe I could subdivide the isochrone into more granular isochrones since I know my origin points are fixed, it might be faster to cache these sub-isochrones rather than doing this calculation on the fly. I thought maybe something like `geoCentroid` would help with that, but it doesn't seem to...

Made a WIP notebook here https://observablehq.com/d/731b8681e60a9432

title: Infinity as the min-y parameter in viewBox????
body:
I'm trying to understand how [this d3 tree code](https://observablehq.com/@d3/tree-component#Tree) works, step-by-step.

There's a step that has me stuck, and I'm hoping someone can explain it.

So, first of all, the code first sets variables dx and x0 , like this:
```
const dx = 10;
let x0 = Infinity;
```
It then creates an svg with a viewBox attribute set like this:
```
const svg = d3.create("svg")
      .attr("viewBox", [-dy * padding / 2, x0 - dx, width, height])
```
The variables `dy`, `padding`, `width` and `height` are set elsewhere in code that I'm omitting.  What I'm hoping someone can help me understand is the part where the min-y parameter is being set to `x0-dx`.

I think (please correct me if this is wrong) that `x0-dx` will evaluate to `Infinity`. 

My understanding is that min-y is the smallest y coordinate in the svg.  And I'm used to setting it to 0, so that if I assign something a positive y coordinate, it will show up somewhere below the top of the viewBox.

But what happens if I set min-y to Infinity????  How do I interpret y coordinates in that case?

Is this some special hack that d3 people like to do?  If so, where is it documented or explained?  I don't see anything in [MDN's article on viewBox ](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/SVG/Attribute/viewBox) about the consequences of setting min-y to Infinity.

Thanks!

### D3 version

When the daylight savings ends there is a gap between the chart bars even though the data is continuous.
In the Code Sandbox (link provided above), you'll be able to toggle between 3 datasets and see the associated charts:

When daylight savings ends there is no gap between the chart bars.

### Actual behavior
When daylight savings ends there is a gap between the chart bars.

title: Executing one transition after another ends
body:
I found example of animating bar charts where you first wait for exit animations and only then apply update animation.
It looks like this
```ts
		const exitTransition = d3.transition().duration(500)
		const updateTransition = exitTransition.transition().duration(500)
		
		groups.selectAll("rect")
			.data(D => D.map(d => {
				type DataWithKey = typeof d & { key: string }
				const dataWithKey = d as DataWithKey;
				dataWithKey.key = D.key;
				return dataWithKey
			}))
			.join(
			(enter) => enter.append('rect')
						.attr("x", d => { return xScale(d.data[0]) ?? 0 })
						.attr("y", this.sizes.height - this.sizes.marginBottom)
						.attr("height", 0)
						.attr("width", d => xScale.bandwidth()),
			(update) => update,
			(exit) => exit.transition(exitTransition)
				    .attr('y', this.sizes.height - this.sizes.marginBottom)
				    .attr('height', 0)
				    .remove()
	    )
			.transition(updateTransition)
			.attr("x", d => { return xScale(d.data[0]) ?? 0 })
			.attr("y", d => y(d[1]))
			.attr("height", d => y(d[0]) - y(d[1]))
			.attr("width", d => xScale.bandwidth())
```

but for some reasons it my case it throws an error like 
`Uncaught (in promise) Error: transition 6 not found`

I've found 2 potential causes of this - 
1. exitTransition ends before updateTransition starts
2. exitTransition fires more than once and each transition can't be reused more than once

Or maybe it is third option, anyway I would appreciate your ideas of what is wrong exactly and potential fixes

title: How to make sure nodes never collide even if there are many nodes
body:
I’m working with a force-directed graph that has a large number of nodes. One quick fix I tried was setting the nodes far apart so they don’t collide. But the downside is that the visualization looks bad (when there are fewer nodes, they’re spaced way too far apart).

Is there a good way to make the layout auto-adjust the node distance based on the number of nodes? I don’t think using a hard-coded rule like if (nodeCount < X) radius = Y is a good long-term solution.

Has anyone found a cleaner or more scalable approach for this?

author: lschneiderman

title: axis function in docs with supposedly mismatched example
body:
Hello,

According to [axis(context)](https://d3js.org/d3-axis#_axis) , the example is the following:

```javascript
svg.append("g")
    .attr("transform", `translate(0,${height - marginBottom})`)
    .call(d3.axisBottom(x));
```

The function is `axis(context)`, where I request to know what is context and how to use the axis function. The example uses `axisBottom`.

title: What to do now that world-atlas package has been archived
body: I'm using D3 along with the `world-atlas` TopoJson package to develop an interactive world map. I understand that `world-atlas` has been archived since 2023, so going forward I'm wondering how I can keep my application up-to-date with the latest geographical changes (e.g. changes to the borders of countries, formation of new countries, etc).
